Originally crafted by Thassilon's founder, the formidable wizard Xin, the Sihedron housed the secrets of Thassilonian magic and stood as a crucial defense against the awakening of the other runelords, should such a scenario arise.

Rovanky Tannery sits at the edge of town, as tanneries do. Larz Rovanky runs it with a demanding efficiency that results in exceptional products and a perpetually thin staff, since Larz regularly fires workers who don't meet his standards and ends up doing most of the work himself.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's tannery — excellent leather goods, long waits, and a perfectionist owner who keeps firing his help for not meeting his standards.
lore.title: Rovanky Tannery

lore.content:
Belimarius was the tenth and final Runelord of Envy, ruler of the Ancient Thassilonian realm of Edasseril. She reigned from -5402 AR until the fall of Thassilon in -5293 AR, when she and the inhabitants of her capital Xin-Edasseril were preserved outside of normal time rather than destroyed by Earthfall.

Very recently, adventurers freed Belimarius and the inhabitants of Xin-Edasseril from this state of captivity, and she has taken up the reins of leadership once again — now as co-ruler of the nation of New Thassilon alongside Runelord Sorshen. She wields Belimarius's Invidious Halberd, a weapon crafted by Xin himself for the previous Runelord of Envy.

The Runelord of Envy specializes in abjuration magic.
lore.summary: The Runelord of Envy and ruler of half of New Thassilon — an ancient archmage who has reawakened from stasis and resumed command of her long-frozen capital Xin-Edasseril.
lore.title: Runelord Belimarius

lore.content:
Runelord Sorshen was the Runelord of Lust in Ancient Thassilon, ruler of the realm of Eurythnia. Her ancient capital was Xin-Eurythnia, now the site of the modern city of Korvosa. Like her peers, she fled into magical stasis when Earthfall threatened the empire.

In recent years, Sorshen awakened and took a strikingly different path from the other returning Runelords — choosing to work against destructive forces rather than seek conquest. She was instrumental in empowering adventurers in their fight against the returning Runelord Alaznist, and invented the Runewaking Ritual, a crucial piece of magic for those hoping to permanently destroy Runelord Zutha.

Sorshen has been collecting the weapons of her fallen fellow Runelords, storing them in her personal demiplane, the Eye of Desire, to protect the world from their power. She now rules a portion of New Thassilon.
lore.summary: The ancient Runelord of Lust — unique among her peers for having chosen reform over conquest, and instrumental in opposing the return of more destructive runelords.
lore.title: Runelord Sorshen

lore.content:
Runelord Xanderghul was the sole holder of the title of Runelord of Pride throughout all of Ancient Thassilon's history — there was only ever one. As the Runelord of Pride, he specialized in illusion magic, whose rune is the Crescent Scepter, and his sin's energy is violet.

Whether Xanderghul survived Earthfall in magical stasis, as some of his peers did, remains unknown.

Perhaps he was too prideful to run.
lore.summary: The one and only Runelord of Pride — a master of illusion who secretly also posed as the god known as the Peacock Spirit, worshipped by rune magic scholars throughout Thassilon.
lore.title: Runelord Xanderghul

lore.content:
Rusthenge is the most significant of the Ancient Thassilonian ruins near the Kindred Coast, situated on an island close to Iron Harbor. Like most Thassilonian ruins scattered across northwestern Avistan, it is a remnant of the empire that was destroyed by Earthfall ten thousand years ago.

The ruin has taken on new and sinister significance thanks to the scholar Meitremar, who has been using research found within Rusthenge to pursue the resurrection of the long-dead demon lord Xar-Azmak — the former ruler of the Abyssal realm of Vlorus. Meitremar operated under the political cover of Runelord Belimarius's government in Xin-Edasseril, lending the power of New Thassilon to his dangerous work.
lore.summary: An Ancient Thassilonian ruin on an island of the Kindred Coast — a site of dangerous historical research that has become the source of a plot to resurrect a dead demon lord.
lore.title: Rusthenge

lore.content:
Sandpoint sits in a natural cove along the cliff-lined Lost Coast of Varisia, roughly fifty miles northeast of Magnimar. It is Varisia's sixth-largest settlement and the only notable stop along this stretch of coast — a town that calls itself the Light of the Lost Coast and mostly believes it. Its reputation for arts, theater, and fine glasswork coexists uneasily with a history of serial murder, goblin raids, giant attacks, Runelord incursions, sinkholes into Thassilonian ruins, and tsunamis.

The town was founded in 4666 AR by four powerful families from Magnimar — the Kaijitsus, Valdemars, Scarnettis, and Deverins — who arrived to find Varisian travelers already using the land for their winter camps. The negotiations that followed went badly, though the resulting scandal was smoothed over, eventually, with a profit-sharing arrangement with the Varisian Council of Magnimar. That arrangement expires in 4726 AR. The town has not forgotten how it started.

Government sits nominally with Mayor Kendra Deverin, a capable diplomat whose personal style of governance has earned her genuine affection. The Sandpoint Town Council shares power with the Mercantile League, the Cathedral, and the increasingly fractious interests of the remaining founding families. The sheriff is Belor Hemlock, a Shoanti man who took the job under terrible circumstances and has kept it well.
lore.summary: The Light of the Lost Coast — a small Varisian town with a long memory and a knack for attracting trouble far above its weight class.
lore.title: Sandpoint

lore.content: Sandpoint's graveyard adjoins the Cathedral grounds and is maintained by Naffer Vosk with meticulous care. It serves as both burial ground and a source of divine magic for those who know to ask. Quiet, orderly, and tended by a man who once lived on the wrong side of the law — it is, somehow, one of the more peaceful places in town.
lore.summary: The town cemetery, attached to the Cathedral — well-kept, quietly sacred, and one of the few places in Sandpoint that has more answers than questions.
lore.title: Sandpoint Boneyard

lore.content: The Sandpoint Boutique stocks a little of everything: equipment, clothing, weapons, tools, books, toys, and art objects, much of it sold on consignment or salvaged from abandoned places. Hayliss Korvaski runs it with brisk Abadaran rectitude and also sells divine spellcasting services — healing chief among them.
lore.summary: Market Street's everything-shop — art, books, toys, tools, and the odd salvaged treasure, under Hayliss Korvaski's exacting eye.
lore.title: Sandpoint Boutique

lore.content:
Sandpoint Cathedral is the town's spiritual center and one of its most prominent buildings. It was rebuilt after the 4702 AR fire that destroyed the original chapel and killed Father Ezakien Tobyn — an event still referred to locally as part of 'the Late Unpleasantness.' The new cathedral honors six deities: Abadar, Desna, Erastil, Gozreh, Sarenrae, and Shelyn, reflecting the diverse beliefs of Sandpoint's population. The Desnan shrine was a condition of the town's founding agreement with the Varisian Council.

Father Abstalar Zantus serves as high priest. Though personally a cleric of Desna, he ministers to followers of all faiths with genuine warmth. The cathedral offers healing magic, religious items, and divine scrolls, and serves as one of the town's two sources of divine spellcasting. The Sandpoint Boneyard is attached to the grounds.
lore.summary: A rare multi-faith cathedral at the heart of town — rebuilt after a devastating fire, and still carrying the weight of what burned.
lore.title: Sandpoint Cathedral

lore.content: The Sandpoint Garrison houses the town watch and serves as the base of operations for Sheriff Belor Hemlock. It is a practical, solid structure befitting a town that has needed to defend itself more than once. Employment at the garrison is one of the more stable jobs in Sandpoint, and Belor runs a professional operation despite the budget constraints of a town this size.
lore.summary: The seat of Sandpoint's law — home to the town watch and the sheriff who keeps this strange little town from coming apart at the seams.
lore.title: Sandpoint Garrison

lore.content: Sandpoint's general store is owned by Ven Vinder, who once took great pride in his business and his family. He's been spending most of his time in a cellar drinking hole since the murder of his eldest daughter. His wife Solsta runs the actual store and is increasingly worried about both her husband and their surviving daughter Shayliss, who has been coming home less and less.
lore.summary: The Vinder family's general store — solid stock, a proprietor drinking himself apart in the cellar, and a wife holding things together by sheer will.
lore.title: Sandpoint General Store

lore.content: The Sandpoint Glassworks was for decades one of the town's great economic engines, operated by the Kaijitsu family whose skill with glass was foundational to Sandpoint's cultural identity. After Ameiko Kaijitsu inherited the building and departed for Minkai, she closed the glassworks rather than deal with persistent rumors that it was haunted by victims of a prior tragedy. It stands empty now — the furnaces cold, the windows dark.
lore.summary: Once the finest glassworks in the region — now closed, locked, and widely believed to be haunted.
lore.title: Sandpoint Glassworks

lore.content:
The Sandpoint Hinterlands is the name for the area surrounding the town of Sandpoint. It encompasses a wide variety of terrain including Brinestump Marsh to the west, the Nettlewood and Mosswood forests, the Tickwood, the rocky Devil's Platter escarpment, the mysterious Turandarok River, and several areas of open moor. Multiple goblin tribes make their homes in the hinterlands.

The Turandarok River is said to lead, eventually, to the First World — an extraordinary claim that has driven many expeditions to madness. Closer to town, the Tickwood offers relatively safe hunting grounds for locals — when one accounts for its fey inhabitants.
lore.summary: The wild and varied countryside surrounding Sandpoint — marshes, forests, moors, and rocky escarpments that conceal goblin tribes, ancient ruins, and stranger things.
lore.title: Sandpoint Hinterlands

lore.content: Almost every lock in Sandpoint passed through the hands of Volioker Briskalberd, a dwarf locksmith of meticulous craft and long memory. He sells locks, fits them, and — for customers he trusts — discusses their weaknesses with professional candor.
lore.summary: Volioker Briskalberd's workshop on Tower Street — the source of nearly every lock in town.
lore.title: Sandpoint Locksmith

lore.content: The Sandpoint Lumber Mill is part of the Scarnetti family's commercial empire, operated under the thumb of its beleaguered manager Ibor Thorn. The mill functions, but Ibor has been suffering increasingly severe nightmares centered on the machinery, and his behavior has become erratic.
lore.summary: One of the Scarnettis' industrial holdings — a working mill with a haunted reputation and a manager whose nightmares are getting worse.
lore.title: Sandpoint Lumber Mill

lore.content: Most days the open marketplace belongs to children's games. On Moonday it fills with local farmers' stalls, and on Fireday it opens to traveling merchants from Magnimar, Galduria, and beyond — the one day a week Sandpoint can buy wares the town itself doesn't stock.
lore.summary: The open square where Festival, Market, and Water Streets meet — children's playground most days, twice a week the town's beating commercial heart.
lore.title: Sandpoint Market

lore.content: Chod Bevuk's establishment is half slaughterhouse, half shopfront, with the day's meat displayed for sale a wall away from where it was dressed. Chod is friendly, loud, and generous with soup bones for regulars.
lore.summary: Market Street's butchery — slaughterhouse in one half, the day's cuts on display in the other.
lore.title: Sandpoint Meat Market

lore.content:
Sandpoint Savories is the town's most popular bakery, and mornings here smell like the best argument for living in Sandpoint. Alma Avertin has run it for decades, and her pastries and cookies are fixtures of every festival and most breakfasts.

Alma herself carries more grief than her warm counter manner shows — she is the twin sister of the sheriff Chopper slew, and her daughter Arika left town for the adventuring life.
lore.summary: The town's most beloved bakery — Alma Avertin's pastries are a civic institution on Main Street.
lore.title: Sandpoint Savories

lore.content: The Sandpoint Shipyard is one of the town's major employers and a legacy of the Valdemar family's original investment in the settlement. With the Valdemars largely departed, the shipyard operates independently, building and repairing vessels for the fishing fleet and occasional larger commissions.
lore.summary: Where Sandpoint's boats get built and repaired — a working industrial facility tied historically to the Valdemar family legacy.
lore.title: Sandpoint Shipyard

lore.content:
The Sandpoint Theater is the town's most celebrated cultural institution and a point of genuine civic pride. Its proprietor, Cyrdak Drokkus, built it into something that can hold its own against the playhouses of Magnimar — a fact he takes great pleasure in, given that he was forced to flee Magnimar in 4700 AR under circumstances he enjoys hinting at without ever quite explaining.

Cyrdak is married to Jasper Korvaski, a retired paladin of Abadar and town planner whose involvement in the Mercantile League has shielded both men from the Scarnettis' persistent disapproval of their marriage.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's crown jewel of culture — a proper playhouse that holds its own against Magnimar's finest, run by a man with a dramatic past and a devoted husband.
lore.title: Sandpoint Theater

lore.content: Sandpoint Town Hall hosts the town's public meetings and houses offices for its councilors, with a secure vault belowground that doubles as the town's bank. The councilors all have businesses and duties of their own, so in practice the building belongs to its clerk: Shayliss Vinder, who knows where every record, key, and favor in Sandpoint's government is kept.
lore.summary: The two-story seat of Sandpoint's government on Main Street — meeting hall above, the town's vault below, and Shayliss Vinder keeping the whole thing running.
lore.title: Sandpoint Town Hall
Sarenrae
lore.summary: The Dawnflower — goddess of healing, redemption, and the sun, whose faith of compassion and temperance is one of the most widely practiced at the Sandpoint Cathedral.
lore.content:
Known to her faithful as the Dawnflower, the Healing Flame, and the Everlight, Sarenrae is a goddess who teaches temperance and patience in all things. Compassion and peace are her greatest virtues, and if enemies of the faith can be redeemed, they should be — though she is not without wrath for those who refuse redemption and continue to cause harm.

Sarenrae is one of the deities whose faith is welcomed at the multi-faith Sandpoint Cathedral. Her worship is widespread throughout the Inner Sea region. Her enemies include the undead goddess Urgathoa, whose portfolio of excess and disease runs counter to everything Sarenrae values.

She is one third of the divine relationship known as the Prismatic Ray, alongside Desna and Shelyn.

Savah Bevaniky runs a well-stocked armory that has seen a notable uptick in business as Sandpoint's reputation as an adventuring hub has grown. She's glad for the coin. She is somewhat less glad about whatever keeps drawing all those adventurers here in the first place.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's best source for weapons and armor — run by a merchant who has noticed, with mixed feelings, how good business has been since the adventurers started arriving.
lore.title: Savah's Armory
Savah Bevaniky
lore.summary: Sandpoint's arms dealer — practical, professional, and quietly worried about why her town needs so many adventurers.
lore.content: Savah runs a clean operation and keeps good stock. She's noticed that every time someone cleans her out of heavy crossbows, something terrible is about to happen to Sandpoint. She is currently well-stocked.

The Scarnetti Consortium is a collection of industrial companies and concerns centered on Sandpoint's lumber and milling industries, all owned and directed by Titus Scarnetti, the head of the Scarnetti family. The Consortium controls the lumber supply that competing interests — especially the now-weakened Valdemars — depend on, and Titus leverages this dependency openly and without apology.

The Consortium has a deeply negative reputation with much of Sandpoint's population, particularly the Varisian community. Evidence of collusion with local criminal elements nearly surfaced publicly when Scarnetti Manor was damaged in a giant raid; Titus managed to suppress most of it, but enough leaked to cause lasting damage to the family's standing. Since then, the Consortium has been rebuilt through secret dealings with Korvosan interests.

The Consortium is distinct from the broader Sandpoint Mercantile League, which it participates in but frequently works against. Its influence rating in town is sharply negative.
lore.summary: An aggressive industrial conglomerate of mills and lumber concerns owned and controlled by Titus Scarnetti — widely regarded as Sandpoint's most unpleasant power broker.
lore.title: The Scarnetti Consortium

lore.content: Scarnetti Manor was damaged during a giant raid and spent several years in disrepair while the family's reputation suffered. Titus Scarnetti has since rebuilt both, and rumors abound about the sudden reversal of fortunes.
lore.summary: The Scarnetti family estate — recently rebuilt after giant-raid damage, and paid for with money from a source Titus would rather not discuss.
lore.title: Scarnetti Manor

lore.content: Scarnetti Mill is one of the family's foundational commercial assets, managed by the perpetually anxious Courrin Whesterwill under conditions that might charitably be described as exploitative.
lore.summary: The Scarnetti family's grain mill — one of their primary commercial operations, run by a man paid barely enough to stay and threatened enough not to leave.
lore.title: Scarnetti Mill
Shayliss Vinder
lore.summary: The Vinders' surviving, yet prodigal, daughter
lore.content: Shayliss has become someone her parents don't quite recognize. She's found faith in Calistria seemingly out of nowhere. Whatever she's doing when she slips out of the secret door by the Turandarok River, it isn't what her mother imagines.

lore.title: Shelyn
lore.summary: The Eternal Rose — goddess of beauty, art, love, and music, whose faith of creative joy and compassion welcomes all who seek beauty in the world.
lore.content:
Shelyn is the goddess of beauty, art, love, and music. She was born the daughter of the spirit-wolf Thron and shares her father with her half-brother Dou-Bral — who, after a long absence and mysterious corruption, returned as the god of pain and shadow, Zon-Kuthon. Shelyn still grieves this transformation and holds love for what her brother once was, even as she opposes what he has become.

Shelyn's faith celebrates creativity and joy in all its forms. She is one third of the divine relationship known as the Prismatic Ray alongside Desna and Sarenrae. Her worshippers are found wherever art is made, and her faith is welcomed at the Sandpoint Cathedral. She is closest among the gods to Brigh, goddess of invention, despite their apparent differences.

lore.title: Solsta Vinder
lore.summary: Ven Vinder's wife — keeping the general store running, keeping the family together, and quietly terrified that she's losing both her husband and her daughter.
lore.content: Solsta manages the store, manages her husband's deterioration, and is starting to manage her fear about where Shayliss keeps disappearing to.

The Curious Goblin is a well-stocked bookshop whose prices reflect its proprietor's priorities — Chask Haladan values the books for their own sake, not for what they'll fetch. His adventuring youth gave him enough of a nest egg to run the shop on his own terms, which means most locals can't afford to shop there regularly. He also offers arcane spellcasting for those who ask.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's bookshop — crammed with tomes, overpriced for the locals, and run by an old scholar who cares more about the books than the business.
lore.title: The Curious Goblin
The Deverin Family
lore.summary: The most respected of Sandpoint's founding families — down-to-earth farmers and brewers whose current leader serves as the town's popular mayor.
lore.content:
The Deverin family is one of the most down-to-earth aristocratic families in Magnimar. Along with their modest town manor in the Naos district of Magnimar, they also own one of Magnimar's most successful breweries. A branch of the family sailed north in 4666 AR to found Sandpoint, and continues to live there today.

The current leader of the Sandpoint branch also serves as the town's mayor. Gaven Deverin, a cousin of the mayor, is the last surviving owner of Two Knight Brewing in Sandpoint.

The Feathered Serpent is the kind of shop that rewards browsing. Vorvashali Voon fills it with curiosities and magical items that seem almost randomly assembled but have a way of being exactly relevant.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's curio shop — stocked with oddities, scrolls, and magical wares by a gregarious proprietor who always seems to know what you need before you do.
lore.title: The Feathered Serpent

lore.content: The Hagfish is a rowdy waterfront tavern popular with sailors, fishers, and people who enjoy a challenge. The house challenge involves drinking from a tank of slime produced by the bar's resident hagfish, Norah. Those who manage it have their names written on the ceiling. The proprietor, Jargie Quinn, has a different story every night about how he lost his leg, and the yarning is half the draw.
lore.summary: A dockside dive bar — home to tall tales, a legendary challenge involving a tank of hagfish slime, and the best excuse to stay drunk on the waterfront.
lore.title: The Hagfish
The Kaijitsu Family
lore.summary: Sandpoint's most exciting founding family — glassmakers from far-off Minkai — whose last daughter left to become an empress, closing the chapter on their presence in town.
lore.content:
The Kaijitsu family traveled across the Crown of the World from Minkai generations ago, eventually settling in Magnimar before becoming one of Sandpoint's founding families as glassmakers and jewelers. The family's profile in Magnimar declined after the death of the family head in a storm in 4687 AR.

Lonjiku Kaijitsu, the family's Sandpoint patriarch, was known for his sharp temper and difficult relationships with his children. His daughter Ameiko became an adventurer and innkeeper, defying all expectations — and eventually ascended to become Empress of Minkai. Lonjiku met a grisly end in the Sandpoint Glassworks during the 4707 AR goblin raids. With Ameiko's departure, the Kaijitsu family has effectively ended its presence in Sandpoint.

Every local has a story about the Pit, and most of them end badly. It is an open shaft in the heart of Devil's Platter, the rocky escarpment a couple of miles southeast of Sandpoint — perpetually fogged below its rim, ringed by jagged stone, and home (the stories insist) to the Sandpoint Devil itself.

Adventurers used to try their luck in its caves semi-regularly. Few came back, and these days most seek their fortunes elsewhere; the most recent company to descend was wiped out, leaving a single survivor. Those who want more than tavern tales are usually pointed at the sage Brodert Quink and his shelves of old books.
lore.summary: A fog-choked shaft sunk into Devil's Platter southeast of town — a byword for danger, and the grave of more than one adventuring company.
lore.title: The Pit

lore.content: The Pixie's Kitten is Sandpoint's open secret — a brothel operated with a discretion that most of the town appreciates and none of the Scarnettis will acknowledge. It offers lodging as well as its primary services, and its proprietor, Kaye Tresarani, is a prominent enough figure in town that her establishment operates without serious harassment.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's brothel — a surprisingly tasteful establishment whose proprietor keeps it safer and quieter than its reputation suggests.
lore.title: The Pixie's Kitten
The Red Bishop
lore.summary: A nameless mothman priest of Pazuzu — the patient architect of two decades of Sandpoint's sorrows.
lore.content:
The Red Bishop shares his true name with no one; it cannot be spoken aloud, only conveyed in an unsettling telepathic sending. He came to the Lost Coast in 4700 AR, drawn by his patron's influence over the region, and found two prizes: an ancient shrine to Pazuzu hidden in the rock of Chopper's Isle, and something far older in the depths of the Pit — a thin place between the Material Plane and Pazuzu's Abyssal realm of High M'vania, held shut by an ancient ward he cannot directly undo.

Preferring slow corruption to swift ruin, he has spent more than twenty years arranging Sandpoint's tragedies: it was the Red Bishop who steered Jervis Stoot to the statuette that made him Chopper, and the Red Bishop whose nightmare-whispers turned the town's heart against Nualia. Every doom Sandpoint has suffered has fed his design — and the seven worst are still to come.

The Rusty Dragon is the beating heart of Sandpoint's social life: the best food in town, a reputation for welcoming adventurers, and a history that includes its former proprietor Ameiko Kaijitsu ascending to become Empress of Minkai. The inn is now managed by Bethana Corwin, a halfling who handles the quieter side of the business, and Daviren Hosk, who handles security and heavy labor while nursing a profound and personal hatred of goblins.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's most popular inn and tavern — warm, welcoming, and still carrying the legend of its famous former owner.
lore.title: The Rusty Dragon
The Scarnetti Family
lore.summary: One of Sandpoint's founding families — conservative, powerful, and deeply unpopular with many locals — who control the town's lumber industry and have a history of shady dealings.
lore.content:
The Scarnetti family controls Sandpoint's mills and lumber industry, making them indispensable to the town's economy despite being widely disliked. Many of Sandpoint's elderly Varisian locals still have not forgotten their part in the tragedy surrounding the town's founding.

The family is headed by Titus Scarnetti and maintains the most conservative, old-school Chelaxian values of any family in town. Recent years have been turbulent: rumors of collusion with local criminals after the Scarnetti Manor was damaged in a giant raid, and Titus allegedly secretly working with Korvosan interests to rebuild the family's fortunes has earned the suspicions of many locals.

lore.title: The Valdemar Family
lore.summary: Once Sandpoint's most prosperous founding family — shipbuilders and carpenters — the Valdemars have fallen on hard times and largely withdrawn from Sandpoint's civic life.
lore.content:
The Valdemar family was once one of the most prosperous of Sandpoint's founding families, generating significant revenue through fishing, shipping, and logging. Found primarily in Magnimar and Sandpoint, the family's fortunes have declined sharply due to competition from the Scarnettis and a series of tragedies.

Ethram Valdemar, the family's Sandpoint patriarch, recently died. The Magnimar branch of the family has shown no interest in maintaining an active presence in Sandpoint, leaving their traditional council seat vacant and their businesses understaffed.

The Way North sells local and regional maps, novelty treasure maps, and the supplies to make your own, all under the proprietorship of Veznutt Parooh — a gnome cartographer and wizard of modest but practical talent who also sells arcane spellcasting services. Veznutt is forever hungry for new maps of anywhere, and pays for good ones.
lore.summary: A cozy map shop on Cliff Street — charts, cartography supplies, and a gnome who will talk routes as long as you let him.
lore.title: The Way North

lore.content: The White Deer offers massive rooms for large parties, as well as the most luxurious accomodations. Its owner, Garridan Viskalai, keeps the place immaculate in an attempt to lure The Next Big Adventuring Party to get some of the spotlight that the Rusty Dragon enjoys. His gruff demeanor undercuts the effort somewhat, but the quality of the rooms speaks for itself. Garridan is the brother of Sheriff Belor Hemlock — a fact neither of them discusses without visible discomfort.
lore.summary: The newest building in town, capitalizing on the influx of flush pursed adventurers coming through town.
lore.title: The White Deer

lore.content:
The Tickwood is a relatively small forested area in the Sandpoint Hinterlands, located on the northern banks of the Turandarok River. It is less than two miles in length, separated from the coastal Shank's Wood to its north by the Lost Coast Road. To its west lie the Ravenroost hills, while Leb's Tors rise to the east.

The Tickwood is a favorite spot for local hunters, as it is thought to be free of goblin tribes, and is home to many deer, wild boars, and the occasional firepelt cougar. Stories in Sandpoint greatly exaggerate the size of the giant ticks that also dwell here, keeping many cautious citizens off the forest paths.

At the heart of the wood stands Akoni's Grove, the court of the dryad Akoni, who presides over the Tickwood's fey inhabitants. A troop of redcaps has recently moved into the area and though they will not establish permanent roots without the dryad's permission, they seem confident they can obtain it one way or another.
lore.summary: A small forest just outside Sandpoint, popular with local hunters — though its fey lord Akoni and a recent redcap incursion make it less safe than it appears.
lore.title: The Tickwood

lore.content:
Titus is the last significant patriarch of the Scarnetti line, operating Sandpoint's lumber industry and nursing grievances against the mayor, the Mercantile League, and the town's general failure to accord him the deference he considers his birthright.

Scarnetti is quite conservative and traditional in his thinking, which often puts him at odds with the other people in town. This disconnect has cost him the race for mayor every time he has run against the more likeable Kendra Deverin, and there is no love lost between the two.

Lately, there have been rumors that Titus has links to certain nefarious individuals in the Sandpoint area—including members of the Sczarni criminal gangs—which have greatly reduced his standing in town.3 The exits of fellow local nobles Ameiko Kaijitsu and the Valdemars have further eroded his influence, leading him to form the Scarnetti Consortium in an attempt to consolidate his power.
lore.summary: Head of the Scarnetti family — a man who has rebuilt his fortune through means he'd prefer to keep quiet, and who hasn't forgiven Sandpoint for not being afraid of him.
lore.title: Titus Scarnetti

lore.content: Turandarok Academy serves as both school and orphanage, funded partly by the town and partly by the personal fortune of its headmaster, the retired adventurer Ilsoari Gandethus. Ilsoari teaches, manages the orphans, and maintains a locked basement full of exotic weapons, strange maps, and monster trophies that he's happy to show adults who ask. The children spin elaborate theories about what's down there. Most of them are wrong. One of them is startlingly close.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's school and orphanage — and, in its locked basement, the private museum of a retired adventurer who has seen things the curriculum doesn't cover.
lore.title: Turandarok Academy

lore.content: Two Knight Brewing produces the ales and lagers that supply most of Sandpoint's taverns and serves them directly in its own taproom. The Alvertin family runs the brewery, and while the beer is reliable and well-regarded, the family has had a difficult few years.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's brewery and taproom — good beer, honest prices, and the Alvertin family keeping things running despite some quiet personal grief.
lore.title: Two Knight Brewing

lore.content: The Valdemar Fish Market sells the local fleet's catch within sight of the water it came from. Turch Sterglus runs it for the Valdemar family with a fisherman's bluntness and a fair scale, and the market sets the rhythm of the waterfront's mornings.
lore.summary: The open-air fish market on Water Street, where the morning catch lands almost straight off the boats.
lore.title: Valdemar Fish Market

lore.content: The Valdemars were one of Sandpoint's four founding families, their fortune built on shipbuilding and carpentry. The family has largely moved on from Sandpoint, and their manor reflects this departure — maintained but no longer a center of power. Their old seat on the town council sits vacant.
lore.summary: The former seat of the Valdemar family — largely vacated now, after Sandpoint's fourth founding family drifted away.
lore.title: Valdemar Manor
Vanda
lore.summary: A former Stonehome acolyte who has not yet fully surrendered to the cult of Xar-Azmak — still wearing her unrusted plate, still carrying a thread of remorse.
lore.content:
Vanda was one of the acolytes of Stonehome, the garrison-church of Gorum in Iron Harbor, who fell under the influence of Meitremar's cult of Xar-Azmak when the Iron Harbor was infiltrated. Unlike the others who fully converted — trading their unrusted steel for the rusted armor and sickles that mark devotion to the dead demon lord — Vanda never made that final surrender.

In the wake of the crisis, Vanda stepped up to lead Stonehome, taking on the responsibilities of a temple left without clear direction after the corruption was purged. She has since thrown herself into rebuilding both the church and the community's trust in it.

She is currently in a situationship with Ashka B'gosh.

Varisia has no central government. Its independent city-states — Magnimar, Korvosa, and the pirate haven of Riddleport — each hold sway over a small collection of towns and villages. The wilds beyond, including most of the Storval Plateau, are claimed by the Shoanti people. The neutral city of Kaer Maga is often discounted from political calculations altogether, and its inhabitants prefer it that way.

The human population is split between Varisians/Shoanti — tribes descended from Thassilon's enslaved workers who developed nomadic cultures of their own — and Chelaxian-descended settlers from the south. 

Ancient Thassilonian ruins are scattered throughout Varisia, relics of an empire destroyed by the cataclysm known as Earthfall ten thousand years ago. These ruins have taken on new significance with the recent return of New Thassilon and The Runelords.
lore.summary: A vast frontier nation on the western coast of Avistan, Varisia is home to ancient Thassilonian ruins, nomadic Varisian and Shoanti peoples, and three major city-states that compete for dominance.
lore.title: Varisia
Ven Vinder
lore.summary: Owner of Sandpoint's general store — a father who lost a daughter to a serial killer and has been losing himself to grief ever since.
lore.content: Ven used to be one of those men who measured their worth in their family. Now he measures it in how many hours he can spend in his cellar drinking before anyone comes looking for him.

Rynshinn Povalli's shop caters to every style Sandpoint wears, and a few it doesn't know it wants yet. Alongside everyday and formal clothing, she fits non-metallic light armor for those whose work runs rougher than most.
lore.summary: Sandpoint's tailor on Prickleback Lane — clothing of all styles, from festival finery to traveling leathers.
lore.title: Vernah's Fine Clothing
Vlorus
lore.summary: The Abyssal realm once ruled by the deceased demon lord Xar-Azmak — a valley of rusted junk and decaying castles, now unclaimed, whose barrier to the Plane of Metal is dangerously thin.
lore.content: Vlorus is the Abyssal realm once ruled by the deceased demon lord Xar-Azmak before his death by Dispater's armies. It is now unclaimed by any power. The valley realm is full of rusted junk and buildings, some as large as the castles that circle the tops of the valley in which the realm is located.

lore.title: Vorvashali Voon
lore.summary: The gregarious proprietor of the Feathered Serpent — a collector and dealer in magical oddities who is currently very interested in clockwork birds.
lore.content: Vorvashali is enthusiastic about almost everything and especially enthusiastic about magical curiosities. Nine out of ten adventurers will tell you one of Vorvashali's items saved their hid, and the tenth didn't make it out of the dungeon.
Bilivar Wheen builds and repairs wagons and wheels, and has begun diversifying into a courier service for parcels and messages around town and along the Lost Coast Road. Honest work, honestly priced.
lore.summary: A wheelwright's shop on River Street, lately branching out into courier work.
lore.title: Wheen's Wagons
The Whistlefangs
lore.summary: A sadistic goblin clan clinging to the Pit's uppermost caves — named for the whistling cries they pipe through holes drilled in their own teeth.
lore.content:
Goblin clans have squatted in the Pit's upper reaches for centuries, and the Whistlefangs are the latest — and nearly the last of their line. Two years ago they held everything above the fog; a soured alliance with two bickering caligni bands and the disastrous arrival of the Devil's Disciples has since reduced them to a fraction of their number, squeezed into the uppermost level against infestations of gremlins.

They were ready to abandon the Pit entirely when a miracle arrived: an undead figure in fiery armor calling itself Ripnugget. The clan's signature is the whistling speech they pipe through drilled teeth — not a true language, but a code of signals they use to coordinate ambushes and defense throughout their warren.

At the height of the Ancient Thassilonian Empire, Emperor Xin crafted seven Alara'quin: magical weapons of extraordinary power, each bound to one of his seven Runelords. Xanderghul's Flawless Hammer was the symbol of office for Xanderghul, the sole and eternal Runelord of Pride.

Xanderghul's domain was illusion magic, and the Hammer reflects this — it is not merely a weapon but a psychic instrument, attuned to pride and the manipulation of emotion. Its most notable property is its reaction to emotional effects: when the wielder would be adversely affected by an emotion, the Hammer attempts to counteract it with a surge of intense pride.

The weapon is currently carried by Jade Inferno, who recovered it and has integrated it into both their spellcasting and their ongoing investigation into New Thassilon's ambitions. Given that Xanderghul himself may have survived Earthfall — no body was ever confirmed — the Hammer's provenance is a subject Jade keeps in mind.

As an Alara'quin, it is an artifact-class item. It cannot be destroyed by conventional means.
lore.summary: One of the seven Alara'quin forged by Emperor Xin — the symbol of office of Xanderghul, Runelord of Pride, now carried by Jade Inferno.
lore.title: Xanderghul's Flawless Hammer

trait.name: Agile
trait.description: The multiple attack penalty you take with this weapon on the second attack on your turn is -4 instead of -5, and -8 instead of -10 on the third and subsequent attacks in the turn.

trait.name: Alchemical
trait.description: Alchemical items contain chemically infused magical energy and tend to be consumable. Improper handling of an alchemical item might cause it to explode, catch fire, or otherwise release dangerous chemicals.

trait.name: Anchoring
trait.description: You can use abilities with the anchoring trait only while your quantum field is activated (unless they are your Signature Spells). If you use the ability on your turn, in addition to their stated effects, they automatically Sustain your quantum field.

trait.name: Attack
trait.description: An action with this trait involves an attack. Attack actions typically use the Strike action or a similar action that targets an enemy's AC.

trait.name: Auditory
trait.description: Auditory actions and effects rely on sound. An action with the auditory trait can be successfully performed only if the creature using the action can speak or otherwise produce the required sounds. A spell or effect with the auditory trait has its effect only if the target can hear it. This applies only to sound-based parts of the effect, as determined by the GM. This is different from a sonic effect, which affects creatures whether or not they can hear it.

trait.name: Backswing
trait.description: You can use the momentum from a missed attack with this weapon to lead into your next attack. After you miss with this weapon on your turn, you gain a +1 circumstance bonus to your next attack with this weapon before the end of your turn.

trait.name: Bard
trait.description: This indicates abilities from the bard class.

trait.name: Bulwark
trait.description: The armor covers you so completely that it provides benefits against some damaging effects. On Reflex saves to avoid a damaging effect, such as a fireball, you add a +3 modifier instead of your Dexterity modifier

trait.name: Cantrip
trait.description: A spell you can cast at will that is automatically heightened to half your level rounded up. Cantrips are a type of spell that don't use spell slots.

trait.name: Champion
trait.description: This indicates abilities from the champion class.

trait.name: Cold
trait.description: Effects with this trait deal cold damage or create ice, snow, or other freezing effects. Creatures and objects with this trait deal cold damage or have a cold theme to their abilities.

trait.name: Composition
trait.description: To cast a composition cantrip or focus spell, you usually use the Performance skill. If the spell includes a verbal component, you must sing, recite, or play an instrument. If it includes a somatic component, you must dance or play an instrument. If it includes a material component, you must use a musical instrument as a focus. Composition spells are a type of focus spell.

trait.name: Concentrate
trait.description: An action with this trait requires a degree of mental concentration and discipline. You can't use an action with the concentrate trait if you're affected by effects that prevent you from concentrating, like being fascinated.

trait.name: Consumable
trait.description: An item with this trait can be used only once. Unless stated otherwise, it's destroyed after activation. Consumable items include alchemical items, ammunition, scrolls, talismans, and most magical oils and potions.

trait.name: Critical Fusion
trait.description: Critical fusion is a trait for combination weapons that grants you an additional option for the critical specialization effect when using the combination weapon's melee version to make a melee attack while the ranged weapon is loaded. If you choose to use one of them, it replaces the melee usage's normal critical specialization effect; you can choose to discharge the ranged weapon to increase the critical hit's momentum or shoot the foe as you attack in melee, dealing 2 additional damage per weapon damage die.

trait.name: Deadly
trait.description: On a critical hit, the weapon adds a weapon damage die of the listed size. Roll this after doubling the weapon’s damage. This increases to two dice if the weapon has a greater striking rune and three dice if the weapon has a major striking rune.

trait.name: Disarm
trait.description: You can use this weapon to knock items out of a creature's grasp. When you use an Attack action with this weapon, you can attempt to disarm the target with the Athletics skill. If you critically succeed at the Athletics check, you can knock the item away from the opponent as normal, or you can grab the item with your attack hand if it's free.

trait.name: Disease
trait.description: An effect with this trait applies a disease to a character. A disease is an affliction other characters can catch from the infected character, making them infected as well.

trait.name: Drug
trait.description: An item with this trait is a drug, which alters a creature's mind and body. Drugs usually have effects that help the user in some way, and separate addiction effects that hinder the user when they're in withdrawal.

trait.name: Electricity
trait.description: Effects with this trait deal electricity damage or create electricity. If an attack with this trait deals damage, all of that damage is electricity damage.

trait.name: Elixir
trait.description: Elixirs are alchemical liquids that contain volatile chemicals that grant the drinker some alchemical benefit. Each elixir is a single dose that takes 1 action to drink (or administer). An elixir's effects typically last for a specified period of time.

trait.name: Emotion
trait.description: This effect alters emotions. Effects with this trait might affect only creatures that have emotions, and usually they alter emotions in some way. Creatures without emotions—typically constructs, mindless undead, and objects—are immune to emotion effects.

trait.name: Evocation
trait.description: A spell that manipulates the raw elemental power of the universe—air, earth, fire, water, or pure magical energy—belongs to the evocation school. Spells that create an object, energy, or a creature out of nothing are also often (though not always) from this school.

trait.name: Extradimensional
trait.description: Items with this trait create an extradimensional storage space. An extradimensional space overlaps with the Material Plane, but the space exists outside any of the dimensions. Using an extradimensional space isn't the same as traveling to another plane.

trait.name: Fatal
trait.description: A fatal weapon is especially effective at dealing critical hits. When you critically succeed at an attack roll with this weapon, you apply the listed weapon specialization effect, and your attack deals one additional die of the listed size. This die applies the effects from all your weapon runes. For example, a pick with fatal d10 deals an additional d10 damage on a critical hit, on top of the doubled damage from the critical hit and on top of the additional damage from weapon specialization.

trait.name: Finesse
trait.description: You can use your Dexterity modifier instead of your Strength modifier on attack rolls using this melee weapon. You still use your Strength modifier when calculating damage.

trait.name: Fire
trait.description: Effects with the fire trait deal fire damage or either conjure or manipulate fire. Those that manipulate fire have no effect in an area without fire.

trait.name: Flourish
trait.description: Actions with this trait are special techniques that require too much exertion to perform a large number in a row. You can use only 1 action with the flourish trait per turn.

trait.name: Focus
trait.description: A spell you can cast by spending a Focus Point, and that is automatically heightened to half your level rounded up.

trait.name: Force
trait.description: Effects with this trait deal force damage or create objects made of magical force. Force effects can damage force barriers such as a wall of force.

trait.name: Fortune
trait.description: A fortune effect beneficially alters how you roll the dice. You can never have more than one fortune effect come into play when you roll a single check, and if multiple fortune effects would apply, you must pick which one to use. If a fortune effect and a misfortune effect would apply to the same roll, the effects cancel each other out and you roll normally.

trait.name: Healing
trait.description: A healing effect restores a creature's body, typically by restoring Hit Points, but sometimes by removing diseases or other debilitating effects.

trait.name: Illusion
trait.description: The illusion school of magic is used to create images, figments, and phantasms to deceive the senses. Illusions can create sensory experiences or have mind-affecting properties. If an illusion creates something insubstantial, like an image, a creature could detect that it's an illusion by physically interacting with it, causing their hand to go through it.

trait.name: Implement
trait.description: A special item that thaumaturges use to channel their esoteric practices. Items with the implement trait can be used as implements by a thaumaturge.

trait.name: Incapacitation
trait.description: An ability with this trait can take a character completely out of the fight or even kill them, and it’s harder to use on a more powerful character. If a spell has the incapacitation trait, any creature of more than twice the spell’s rank treats the result of their check to prevent being incapacitated by the spell as one degree of success better, or the result of any check the spellcaster made to incapacitate them as one degree of success worse. If any other effect has the incapacitation trait, a creature of higher level than the item, creature, or hazard generating the effect gains the same benefits.

trait.name: Invested
trait.description: A character can wear only 10 magical items that have the invested trait. None of the magical effects of the item apply if the character hasn't invested the item, though they still gain any normal benefits from wearing or carrying the physical item (like a hat keeping rain off their head).

trait.name: Light
trait.description: Effects with this trait create light. Light effects have a specified range, and some can target objects that then emanate light, potentially with a different range. Light with a larger radius can illuminate creatures across a greater distance, making them easier to detect using sight. For instance, an effect that creates dim light in a 20-foot radius provides illumination sufficient to reduce creatures' Stealth modifier from darkness from a +2 circumstance bonus to a +1 circumstance bonus anywhere in that 20 foot area.

trait.name: Magical
trait.description: Something with the magical trait is infused with magical energies not tied to a specific tradition of magic. A magical item radiates a magic aura infused with its effect.

trait.name: Manipulate
trait.description: You must physically manipulate an item or make gestures to use an action with this trait. Creatures without a suitable appendage can't perform actions with this trait. Manipulate actions often trigger reactions.

trait.name: Melee
trait.description: This weapon or attack is used in melee, meaning that it can be used only against creatures within reach of the wielder. Most melee weapons have a reach of 5 feet, but some weapons have different reach values.

trait.name: Mental
trait.description: A mental effect can alter the target's mind. It has no effect on an object or a mindless creature.

trait.name: Metal
trait.description: Effects with the metal trait conjure or manipulate metal. Those that manipulate metal have no effect in an area without metal. Creatures with this trait consist primarily of metal or have a connection to magical metal.

trait.name: Monk
trait.description: Monks can use abilities with this trait, and those with this trait can be found in the monk class.

trait.name: Nonlethal
trait.description: Attacks with this weapon are nonlethal, and are used to knock creatures unconscious instead of killing them. They do not apply the dying condition or the normal penalties for attacking nonlethally with a weapon that doesn't have this trait.

trait.name: Oil
trait.description: This item is a container of oil that can be applied to an object or creature. Applying a vial of oil typically takes 1 action. The effects apply to the first target hit with the oil, and the substance becomes inert immediately thereafter.

trait.name: Parry
trait.description: This weapon can be used defensively to block attacks. While wielding this weapon, if your proficiency with it is trained or better, you can spend a single action to position your weapon defensively, gaining a +1 circumstance bonus to AC until the start of your next turn.

trait.name: Poison
trait.description: An effect with this trait delivers a poison or deals poison damage. An item with this trait is poisonous and might cause an affliction.

trait.name: Potion
trait.description: A potion is a magical liquid activated when you drink it, which uses it up. Potions have the consumable trait and a variety of subtypes based on their effects. They typically can be used only once.

trait.name: Ranged
trait.description: This weapon is used for making a ranged attack and has a maximum range in feet.

trait.name: Reach
trait.description: This weapon is long and can be used to attack creatures up to 10 feet away instead of only adjacent creatures. For creatures that already have reach with the limb or limbs that wield the weapon, the weapon increases their reach by 5 feet.

trait.name: Sanctified
trait.description: If you are holy or unholy, your sanctified actions and spells gain the same trait.

trait.name: Scroll
trait.description: Scrolls contain the necessary magic to cast a particular spell without using your spell slots. You must hold the scroll in one hand and cast the spell as if using the Cast a Spell activity, except you perform verbal, somatic, and material components while reading directly from the scroll.

trait.name: Shove
trait.description: You can use this weapon to push a creature away from you. When you use an Attack action with this weapon, you can attempt to Shove the target using the Athletics skill. If you successfully Shove a target with this weapon, you can move the target an additional 5 feet back, for a total of 10 feet. This doesn't count as a move action for the target.

trait.name: Sonic
trait.description: An effect with the sonic trait functions only if it makes sound, meaning it has no effect in an area of silence or in a vacuum. This is different from an auditory effect, which relies on the target being able to hear it.

trait.name: Spirit
trait.description: Effects with this trait can affect creatures with spiritual essence and might deal spirit damage. A creature with this trait is defined by its spiritual essence. Spirit creatures often lack a material form.

trait.name: Talisman
trait.description: A talisman is a small object affixed to armor, a shield, or a weapon. Each talisman holds a sliver of magical energy that can be activated for a specific benefit. A talisman can be activated only once, after which it burns out and becomes useless. If a talisman is removed, it keeps its magical energy for 24 hours before burning out.

trait.name: Tech
trait.description: Items with this trait incorporate things like electronics, computer systems, integrated power sources, and/or a comm unit. They cannot use archaic runes.

trait.name: Thrown
trait.description: You can throw this weapon as a ranged attack; it is a ranged weapon when thrown. A thrown weapon adds your Strength modifier to damage just like a melee weapon does. When this trait appears on a melee weapon, it also includes the range increment.

trait.name: Unarmed
trait.description: An unarmed attack uses your body rather than a manufactured weapon. An unarmed attack isn't a weapon, though it's categorized with weapons for weapon groups, and it might have weapon traits. Since it's part of your body, an unarmed attack can't be Disarmed. It also doesn't take up a hand, though a fist or other grasping appendage generally works like a free-hand weapon.

trait.name: Uncommon
trait.description: Something of uncommon rarity requires special training or comes from a particular culture or part of the world. Some character choices give access to uncommon options, and the GM can choose to allow access for anyone. Less is known about uncommon items than common items, and they're generally not available except from special merchants.

trait.name: Visual
trait.description: A visual effect can affect only creatures that can see it. This applies only to parts of the effect that would require seeing it, as determined by the GM.

trait.name: Volley
trait.description: This ranged weapon is less effective at close distances. Your attacks against targets that are at a distance within the listed range take a –2 penalty.

trait.name: Wand
trait.description: A wand contains a single spell which can be cast once per day. Most wands are made from wood, but some rare materials like ivory, metal, and even crystal can be used.

trait.name: Zone
trait.description: These actions apply an additional effect to your quantum field. You may apply effects of a zone only to a quantum field that doesn't have a zone effect.

passives.name: Implement's Empowerment
passives.benefit: The power of your implement can also be turned to the more common task of combat, its power adding to and amplifying the effects of runes and other magical empowerments. When you Strike, you can trace mystic patterns with an implement you're holding to empower the Strike, causing it to deal 2 additional damage per weapon damage die. Channeling the power requires full use of your hands. You don't gain the benefit of implement's empowerment if you are holding anything in either hand other than a single one-handed weapon, other implements, or esoterica, and you must be holding at least one implement to gain the benefit.

exploits.name: Personal Antithesis
exploits.description: You improvise a custom weakness on a creature by forcefully presenting and empowering a piece of esoterica that repels it on an individual level; for instance, against a tyrant, you might procure a broken chain that once held a captive. This causes the target creature, and only the target creature, to gain a weakness against your unarmed and weapon Strikes equal to 2 + half your level.

exploits.name: Mortal Weakness
exploits.description: After identifying a creature's weakness, you use a thematically resonant bit of esoterica to attune your attacks to your discovery. Your unarmed and weapon Strikes activate the highest weakness you discovered with Exploit Vulnerability, even though the damage type your weapon deals doesn't change. This damage affects the target of your Exploit Vulnerability, as well as any other creatures of the exact same type, but not other creatures with the same weakness. For example, when fighting a pack of werewolves you might use silver shavings or crushed moonstone to deal damage that applies their weakness to silver to your attacks against any of the werewolves, but you wouldn't apply this damage to any other monsters with a weakness to silver.

damageRiders.label: Implement's Empowerment
damageRiders.note: Hold an implement; hands free except a single one-handed weapon, other implements, or esoterica

actions.name: Hunt Prey
actions.description:
You designate a single creature as your prey and focus your attacks against that creature. You gain a +2 circumstance bonus to Perception checks when you Seek your prey and a +2 circumstance bonus to Survival checks when you Track your prey.

You also ignore the penalty for making ranged attacks within your second range increment against the prey you're hunting.

You can have only one creature designated as your prey at a time. This designation lasts until your next daily preparations.

feats.name: Ranger Dedication
feats.description: You become trained in Survival; if you were already trained in Survival, you instead become trained in another skill of your choice. You become trained in ranger class DC. You can use the Hunt Prey action.

feats.name: Hard to Fool
feats.description: You're accustomed to seeing through shifting darkness and bewildering magic. You gain a +1 circumstance bonus on Perception checks against illusions as well as to Will saves against illusions and shadow effects.

feats.name: Bargain Hunter
feats.description: You can Earn Income (page 228) using Diplomacy, spending your days hunting for bargains and reselling at a profit. You can also spend time specifically sniffing out a great bargain on an item; this works as if you were using Earn Income with Diplomacy, except instead of gaining money, you purchase the item at a discount equal to the money you would have gained, gaining the item for free if your earned income equals or exceeds its cost.

feats.name: Charming Liar
feats.description: Your charm allows you to win over those you lie to. When you get a critical success using the Lie action, the target's attitude toward you improves by one step, as though you'd succeeded at using Diplomacy to Make an Impression. This works only once per conversation, and if you critically succeed against multiple targets using the same Lie, you choose one creature's attitude to improve.

feats.name: Dubious Knowledge
feats.description: You're a treasure trove of information, but not all of it comes from reputable sources. When you fail (but don't critically fail) a Recall Knowledge check using any skill, you learn a bit of true knowledge and a bit of erroneous knowledge, but you don't have any way to differentiate which is which.

feats.name: Quick Squeeze
feats.description: You Squeeze 5 feet per round (10 feet on a critical success), rather than 5 feet per minute (10 feet on a critical success).

feats.name: Diverse Lore
feats.description: Your wandering studies mean you've heard rumors or theories about almost every topic... though admittedly, your sources aren't always the most reliable. You can take a –2 penalty to your check to Recall Knowledge with Esoteric Lore to Recall Knowledge about any topic, not just the usual topics available for Esoteric Lore. Additionally, when you succeed at your check to Exploit a Vulnerability, compare the result of your Esoteric Lore check to the DC to Recall Knowledge for that creature; if that number would be a success or a critical success, you gain information as if you had succeeded at the Recall Knowledge check.

feats.name: Familiar
feats.description: You make a pact with a creature that serves you and assists you; you gain a familiar.

actions.name: Spell-Signature Reading
actions.description: You study the magical energy of a spellcaster you can see within 30 feet. Make a secret Arcana check against the spellcaster's Will DC. On a success, you learn if the creature is casting the spell as a spontaneous, prepared, or innate spellcaster, if they can cast spells from spell slots, and what attribute they use to cast spells. If a creature can cast a spell from multiple sources, you learn both.

feats.name: Spell-Signature Reading
feats.description: Whenever any caster performs magic, no matter how meager, it creates a vibrant display of runes circling around an intricate glyph known as a spell signature. As a learned student of the arcane yourself, you know all too well what information can be gleaned from such runes, such as one's personality or skill level with magic. You learn if the creature is casting the spell as a spontaneous, prepared, or innate spellcaster, if they can cast spells from spell slots, and what attribute they use to cast spells. If a creature can cast a spell from multiple sources such as having innate spells and prepared spells both, you learn both.

feats.name: Scroll Thaumaturgy
feats.description: Your multidisciplinary study of magic means you know how to activate the magic in scrolls with ease. You can activate scrolls of any magical tradition, using your Thaumaturge class DC for the resulting spell's DC. When you do, you choose which of its possible traditions to use when you activate it. You can draw and activate scrolls with the same hand holding an implement, much like you can for esoterica.

feats.name: Animal Companion
feats.description: You gain the service of a young animal companion that travels with you and obeys simple commands. When you Hunt Prey, your animal companion gains the action's benefits.

feats.name: Discrete Inquiry
feats.description: You are subtle in your efforts to learn the things you need to know. When Gathering Information, you can hide the true subject of your inquiry among other topics of little interest to you without increasing the difficulty of the check or taking more time to Gather Information. Anyone trying to Gather Information to determine if someone else was asking about the topic in question must exceed your Deception DC or the normal DC to Gather Information about your inquiries, whichever is higher, or else they don't learn of your efforts.

degrees.Critical Success: You remember the creature's weaknesses, and as you empower your esoterica, you have a flash of insight that grants even more knowledge about the creature. You learn all of the creature's resistances, weaknesses, and immunities, including the amounts of the resistances and weaknesses and any unusual weaknesses or vulnerabilities, such as what spells will pass through a golem's antimagic. You can exploit either the creature's mortal weakness or personal antithesis. Your unarmed and weapon Strikes against the creature also become magical if they weren't already.
degrees.Success: You recall an important fact about the creature, learning its highest weakness (or one of its highest weaknesses, if it has multiple with the same value) but not its other weaknesses, resistances, or immunities. You can exploit either the creature's mortal weakness or personal antithesis. Your unarmed and weapon Strikes against the creature also become magical if they weren't already.
degrees.Failure: Failing to recall a salient weakness about the creature, you instead attempt to exploit a more personal vulnerability. You can exploit only the creature's personal antithesis. Your unarmed and weapon Strikes against the creature also become magical if they weren't already.
degrees.Critical Failure: You couldn't remember the right object to use and become distracted while you rummage through your esoterica. You become flat-footed until the beginning of your next turn.

actions.name: Exploit Vulnerability
actions.description: You scour your experiences and learning to identify something that might repel your foe. You retrieve an object from your esoterica with the appropriate supernatural qualities, then use your implement to stoke the remnants of its power into a blaze. Select a creature you can see and attempt an Esoteric Lore check against a standard DC for its level, as you retrieve the right object from your esoterica and use your implement to empower it. You gain the following effects until you Exploit Vulnerabilities again.

familiar.name: Lazarus
familiar.communication: Empathic link with Ashka within 1 mile
familiar.description: A Squox is an exotic creature resembling a cross between a squirrel and a fox. Little is known about them in Golarion, not even where they are native to; the only clue to their origin is the fact that they can most often be tracked back to Elven traders though where they procure the creatures in the first place remains a mystery.

abilities.name: Manual Dexterity
abilities.description: Your familiar can use up to two of its limbs as if they were hands to use manipulate actions.

abilities.name: Threat Display
abilities.description: Your familiar helps you convey wordless threats through body language. Whenever you attempt an Intimidation check to Demoralize a creature, if your familiar is within 30 feet of your target and can act, it accompanies you with snarls, hisses, or raising its hackles. If it can do so, you don't take the normal –4 penalty on the Intimidation check if your target doesn't understand the language you're speaking.

abilities.name: Squox Tricks
abilities.description: A squox can Disarm and Trip creatures with its tail using Acrobatics instead of Athletics, even if it doesn't have a free hand. A squox can attempt to Disarm or Trip off-guard creatures up to two sizes larger than it and gains a +2 circumstance bonus to the attempt.

animalCompanion.name: Zevira
animalCompanion.support: Zevira shrouds your foes in shadow when you leave an opening. Until the start of your next turn, when you damage a creature with a Strike and that creature is within reach of your Shadow Hound, both you and your hound become Concealed to the creature until the end of your next turn.

stances.name: Dragon Stance
stances.description: You enter the stance of a dragon and make powerful leg strikes like a lashing dragon's tail. You can make dragon tail attacks that deal 1d10 bludgeoning damage. They are in the brawling group and have the backswing, nonlethal, and unarmed traits.
stances.restrictions: You can use this action only while in Dragon Stance.

feats.name: Dragon Spit
feats.description: Many Tian-Dan claim to have dragon blood in their veins, and in your case, this is true—you can spit.

actions.name: Dragon Stance
actions.description: You enter the stance of a dragon and make powerful leg strikes like a lashing dragon's tail. You can make dragon tail attacks that deal 1d10 bludgeoning damage. They are in the brawling group and have the backswing, nonlethal, and unarmed traits. While in Dragon Stance, you can ignore the first square of difficult terrain while Striding.

strikes.name: Dragon Tail Strike
strikes.description: You swing your leg through the air like a dragon swiping with its tail. You can only use this when you are in Dragon Stance.

feats.name: Dragon Stance
feats.description: You enter the stance of a dragon and make powerful leg strikes like a lashing dragon's tail. You can make dragon tail attacks that deal 1d10 bludgeoning damage. They are in the brawling group and have the backswing, nonlethal, and unarmed traits.

feats.name: Qi Spells
feats.description: You can tap into your inner qi to cast special spells called qi spells. You gain a focus pool of 1 Focus Point. You learn the ki strike and wholeness of body qi spells.

actions.name: Battle Medicine
actions.description: You can patch up wounds, even in combat. Attempt a Medicine check with the same DC as for Treat Wounds and restore the corresponding amount of HP; this doesn’t remove the wounded condition. As with Treat Wounds, you can attempt checks against higher DCs if you have the minimum proficiency rank. The target is then immune to your Battle Medicine for 1 day. This does not make them immune to, or otherwise count as, Treat Wounds.

feats.name: Battle Medicine
feats.description: You can patch up yourself or an adjacent ally, even in combat. Attempt a Medicine check with the same DC as for Treat Wounds and restore a corresponding amount of Hit Points. This doesn't remove the wounded condition.

feats.name: Godless Healing
feats.description: You recover more Hit Points from effects that only grant healing. You gain a +2 circumstance bonus to Hit Points regained by effects that only grant healing.

feats.name: Quick Repair
feats.description: You can Repair items quickly. You can Repair items in half the normal time (minimum 1 minute). If you're a master in Crafting, you can Repair items in one-quarter the normal time (minimum 1 minute).

strikes.name: Ravager Claw
strikes.description: Rend their flesh with your draconic claws.

strikes.name: Ravager Fang
strikes.description: Bite into your foe with magically sharp dragon teeth.

actions.name: Take Flight
actions.description: You've grown a pair of scaled wings, but they're taking some getting used to. Once per round, you can fly up to 15 feet. If you aren't on solid ground at the end of this movement, you fall.

feats.name: Draconic Ravager
feats.description: You have begun to focus your draconic magic from within into your body, giving you dragon-like features like claws, sharpened teeth, and scales, making you a fearsome sight to behold. You gain claw (1d6) and jaw (1d8) unarmed attacks that have the agile and finesse traits. Your eyes have also sharpened, giving you Darkvision.

actions.name: Staunch Bleeding

variants.note: Treat Wounds at the normal DC

actions.description: As a single action you can Treat Wounds to stop someone from bleeding; if you use two actions the DC is reduced by 10.

feats.name: Staunch Bleeding
feats.description: You know how to pack an injury properly to stop bleeding.

reactions.name: Deflect Projectile
reactions.trigger: You are the target of a physical ranged attack, are aware of the attack, are not off-guard, and have a free hand
reactions.description: You gain a +4 circumstance bonus to AC against the triggering attack. If the attack misses, you have deflected it. You cannot use this to deflect unusually massive ranged projectiles (such as boulders or ballista bolts).

feats.name: Deflect Projectile
feats.description: Your reflexes are so sharp, you can swat projectiles out of the air.

reactions.name: Wing Deflection
reactions.trigger: You are the target of a physical attack, are aware of the attack, and are not off-guard
reactions.description: You gain a +2 circumstance bonus to AC against the triggering attack.

feats.name: Wing Deflection
feats.description: You can protect yourself from attacks with your wings.

feats.name: Mortal Healing
feats.description: You grant greater healing when the gods don't interfere. When you roll a success to Treat Wounds for a creature that hasn't regained Hit Points from divine magic in the past 24 hours, you get a critical success instead.

actions.name: Flurry of Blows
actions.description: Make two unarmed Strikes. If both hit the same creature, combine their damage for the purpose of resistances and weaknesses. Apply your multiple attack penalty to the Strikes normally. As a flourish ability, you can use Flurry of Blows only once per turn.

powers.name: Erode
powers.description: You touch a creature, ushering them towards the harsh embrace of death. Make a melee spell attack roll against a foe in reach, dealing 2d10 (+1d10 per level) void damage.

degrees.Critical Success: The target succumbs to the call of the Void and takes double damage
degrees.Failure: The target takes damage equal to 2 + half your level
degrees.Critical Failure: The target resists, taking no damage

powers.name: Rust Cloud
powers.description: Rust pours out of your mouth and eyes, creating a cloud of rust that extends 20-feet in every direction. All creatures inside the cloud are Concealed, and creatures outside the cloud are concealed to them. You continue producing this cloud until you spend 2 actions to Dismiss the effect, 10 minutes pass, or you fall unconscious, whichever comes first.

special.name: Shrouded
special.description: When a creature is shrouded, all light within 20 feet of them is lowered in intensity by one step (bright to dim, dim to dark).

powers.name: Gloaming Backstab
powers.description: You reach into the shadows and pull out a blade of pure darkness. You deal 2d6 (+1d6 per level) void damage to a foe within your reach with a Melee Spell Attack. If you're hidden, you deal an additional 2d4 (+1d4 per level) percision damage. You become Shrouded until the end of your turn.

powers.name: Chains of Rust
powers.description: Rusted chains erupt from the ground, ensnaring your foes. Choose one of your space's four sides; all foes adjacent to you along that side take 2d6 (+1d6 per level) bludgeoning damage with a basic Fortitude Save.

degrees.Failure: All movement is difficult terrain for the creature until the start of your next turn.
degrees.Critical Failure: The creature is immobilized until the start of your next turn.

powers.name: Addling Blast
powers.description: You blast a cone of disorienting mental energy to muddle your foes' minds and throw them off guard. You deal 1d4 (+1d4 per level) mental damage to all creatures in a 15-foot cone with a basic Will save.

degrees.Success: The creature is Off-Guard until the start of their next turn
degrees.Failure: The creature is Off-Guard and Stupefied 1 until the end of your next turn
degrees.Critical Failure: The creature is Off-Guard and Stupefied 2 until the end of your next turn

powers.name: Psychic Thrust
powers.description: You thrust psychic energy into a foe from a distance to overwhelm their mind. Make a ranged Spell Attack against a foe in range, dealing 2d6 (+1d6 per level) mental damage

degrees.Critical Success: You hit a mental weak spot, dealing double damage and inflicting Stupefied 3 until the end of your next turn
degrees.Success: The enemy takes regular damage and is Stupefied 2 until the end of your next turn
degrees.Failure: The enemy takes no damage, and is Stupefied 1 until the end of your next turn
degrees.Critical Failure: The enemy's psyche is too strong and they are unaffected

special.name: Limned
special.description: A limned creature is surrounded by light, causing it to glow like a torch, outlining it and making its position obvious. A limned creature cant normally become concealed, though if the creature would become invisible it will become concealed instead. Attempts at stealth while Limned take a -4 penalty.

powers.name: Stupefying Inception
powers.description: You touch a foe and release overflowing mental energy into their vulnerable mind, damaging them and briefly weakening their psychic defenses. Make a Spell Attack against an enemy; you deal 2d8 (+1d8 per level) mental damage to them.

degrees.Critical Success: You deal double damage, and they are Stupefied 3 until the end of your next turn
degrees.Success: You deal regular damage, and they are Stupefied 2 until the end of your next turn
degrees.Failure: They take no damage, but are Stupefied 1 until the end of your next turn
degrees.Critical Failure: They resist your psychic assault and take no damage

powers.name: Shining Guidance
powers.description: You call forth a guiding light to surround your foe and guide your attacks toward them. Choose an enemy in range; you deal 2d4 (+1d4 per level + half your level) fire damage with a Basic Fortitude Save.

degrees.Critical Success: The creature resists entirely and is unaffected
degrees.Success: The enemy is Limned until the end of your next turn. You can choose to deal no fire damage rather than half; if you do, you and your allies get a +1 circumstance bonus to attack rolls against them until the end of your next turn, as long as it remains limned
degrees.Failure: The foe is Limned until the end of your next turn. You and your allies get a +1 circumstance bonus on attack rolls against the creature until the end of your next turn, as long as it remains limned
degrees.Critical Failure: The foe is limned for two rounds (ending at the end of your turn). During that time, as long as it remains limned, you and your allies gain a +1 circumstance bonus to attack rolls against them

special.name: Charged
special.description: Using electric Eld abilities gives you Charge; while charged, you are off-guard against attacks that deal electricity damage and take a -2 on saves against electric effects. The charged condition lasts until the encounter ends, you take electric damage, or you Discharge to empower another Electric Eld Power.

powers.name: Electric Surge
powers.description: You send a surging current of electricity in front of you, shocking creatures in a line. You deal 1d4 (+1d4 per level) electric damage to all creatures in a 20ft line with a Basic Reflex Save.
